proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence describes Taito Milestones 4 as a retro arcade compilation containing ten different games across multiple distinct genres and play styles: sports minigames, martial arts/fighting, block-breaking/paddle action, platform action, shoot 'em up, maze/ball-rotation play, and beat-'em-up style action. The included games do not converge on a single stable gameplay identity that maps cleanly to one Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The Metacritic description is also contradictory/noisy, referring to an unrelated puzzle game ('ZOK'), which further weakens title-level archetype assignment. Because this is a mixed-genre compilation rather than one coherent game loop, classification into a single archetype would be misleading.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct neighboring entry in the same Taito arcade compilation series, with the same curated multi-genre retro collection format.
Another installment built around a bundle of classic Taito arcade games spanning shooters, action, and other short-session coin-op styles.
The original release in the same anthology line, sharing the same preservation-first structure and classic arcade pick-up-and-play focus.
A recognizable arcade anthology built around emulated classic coin-op games, score attack appeal, and genre variety rather than a single modern campaign.
Another classic arcade compilation offering a recognizable cross-section of older action and shooter games designed for short runs and replayable high-score play.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently identify the game as a single-player point-and-click adventure centered on exploring scenes, finding hidden clues, and solving intricate puzzles to progress an authored story. The text repeatedly emphasizes puzzle-solving, cursor-driven interaction, and narrative progression, while giving no indication of combat. That strongly fits the taxonomy's hidden_object_puzzle lane, even if the copy leans more toward point-and-click adventure phrasing than explicit hidden-object labeling.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: whimsical single-player point-and-click adventure with guided exploration, environmental puzzles, mini-games, and non-combat story progression.
Strong mystery adventure comparison built on scene investigation, clue discovery, dialogue, and puzzle-led progression in a linear single-player structure.
Classic hotspot-driven point-and-click structure with inventory logic, episodic cases, and no combat makes it a good mechanical neighbor despite a more comedic case-based format.
Story-driven detective adventure with clue gathering and puzzle solving, but it leans more into dialogue, deduction, and narrative choice than the anchor’s environmental puzzle exploration.
Pure point-and-click progression driven by scene inspection, inventory use, and dense environmental puzzle solving without combat.

title: Karigurashi Ren'ai: Living on Borrowed Love

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions repeatedly and explicitly call the game an 'interactive comedy visual novel' where 'your choices matter' and 'shape the protagonist's relationships' with multiple heroines. The evidence consistently supports a single-player, campaign-style, non-combat experience centered on branching authored narrative and dialogue/relationship choices, which is a strong fit for the visual_novel archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another comedy-first romance visual novel with frequent dialogue-driven scenes, heroine routes, and player choices steering relationship outcomes.
Modern romance VN focused on dating heroines through route selection, lighthearted slice-of-life writing, and branching romantic progression.
School-life romance visual novel built around choosing a heroine and advancing through comedic, choice-based relationship routes.
Romantic slice-of-life VN with a shared living setup, multiple heroine paths, and a strong emphasis on character interactions over mechanical gameplay.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Renai Karichaimashita: Koikari - Love For Hire
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another route-based romance VN with a playful tone, multiple heroines, and choices that determine which romantic storyline you pursue.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define GRIDbeat! as a rhythm-fueled cyber-dungeon crawler where movement, combat, and interaction all occur on the beat. The core loop is beat-synced traversal through maze-like levels, solving puzzles, battling security programs, and defeating major AI boss encounters. This strongly supports rhythm_game as the primary identity, with the rhythm-timing input layer driving both traversal and combat across a single-player campaign structure.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest gameplay match: beat-synced movement and attacks, dungeon crawling, run-based progression, and high-pressure rhythm combat align extremely closely.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Cadence of Hyrule: Crypt of the NecroDancer Featuring The Legend of Zelda
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Built directly on NecroDancer-style square-to-square rhythm movement and combat, with a somewhat more adventure-forward overworld structure but still a very close fit.
It shares rhythm-timed dungeon crawling and ability progression, but its looter-shooter emphasis and co-op-friendly structure make it feel less like GRIDbeat!'s tighter crawler/boss model.
Strong beat-enforced action and boss progression overlap, but the FPS perspective, shooter combat, and roguelike room-clearing identity move it away from the anchor's top-down dungeon-crawl feel.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Metronomicon: Slay the Dance Floor
staged_neighbors.requested_title: The Metronomicon: Slay The Dance Floor
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Rhythm-driven combat with dungeon-run structure, party progression, and boss encounters rather than pure score attack.

title: Neopets - Mega Mini Games Collection - The Neopian Arcade Odyssey

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a retro-inspired collection of 26 remastered Neopets mini-games with Arcade/Challenge/Endless modes, high-score chasing, unlockables, and some local multiplayer. However, the bundle is intentionally heterogeneous and the text does not establish a single dominant gameplay identity that cleanly maps to one allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The current computed signals that inferred first-person shooter traits are clearly spurious relative to the actual description. 'Party game' is the closest possible fit, but the evidence emphasizes an arcade mini-game compilation with mostly solo/high-score play rather than a clearly social, match-driven party-game structure. Because the package is multi-mode and cross-genre without a strong singular archetype in the allowed list, classification should be rejected rather than forced.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Fast, varied mini-game-driven play built around short sessions, replayability, and local multiplayer party appeal.
Arcade-style challenge structure focused on brief runs, score/time optimization, and replaying classic-style activities.
Retro compilation format with many distinct arcade-style games, modernized presentation, and high-score chasing.
Accessible classic arcade collection centered on short-session gameplay, recognizable variety, and leaderboard-style replay value.
Curated retro collection that modernizes a library of older games for contemporary play while preserving pick-up-and-play arcade structure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Classification is supported primarily by explicit source labeling and the strongly story-led synopsis. Metacritic directly labels the game as a 'Visual Novel,' which is the clearest gameplay-identity signal in the bundle. The stored descriptions focus on protagonist, romance, betrayal, and outcome/fate rather than action systems, traversal, or combat mechanics, which fits a narrative-first visual novel structure. The current taxonomy debug payload also already extracted 'combat_presence: none' and 'narrative_structure: authored_linear' from stored evidence, reinforcing a non-combat, linear story experience. 'Otome' framing and the romance-heavy language support interpersonal drama within an anime-styled single-player campaign format. Some fingerprint fields remain inferred at low granularity because concrete UI/mechanical details are sparse, but the core archetype is well supported.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Otome VN focused on war, rival factions, romance routes, and a heroine navigating martial conflict and loyalty.
Choice-driven otome visual novel with a combat-era Japanese setting, betrayal, danger, and romance paths.
Classic historical otome VN built around factional conflict, relationship routes, and dramatic story choices.
Story-heavy otome visual novel with romance routes and a heroine caught up in supernatural and political turmoil.
Another prominent otome VN emphasizing route-based romance, narrative choices, and heroine-centered worldbuilding.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game around skill-based block-breaking 'Rebound challenges' where the player keeps a walnut in play, manipulates shot angles with timed tail-whips, clears every block from stages, chases long volleys/high scores, and uses power-up interactions across varied obstacle layouts. That strongly supports a pinball-like arcade core rather than RPG or sports identity. The same copy also describes an interconnected six-floor tower between challenge stages, with environmental puzzles, unlocked paths, NPC help requests, secrets, bosses, and a linear mystery campaign, which supports metroidvania as a secondary influence rather than the primary loop. Evidence is solid from repeated store descriptions, but some specifics like exact camera/perspective and traversal verbs are less explicit, so confidence is moderate-high rather than maximal.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct block-breaking adventure hybrid with power-ups, bosses, world progression, exploration, and light quest/economy structure; this matches the anchor's core loop most closely.

Very close audience fit: pinball-like ball control fused with traversable world exploration, quests, secrets, and ability-gated progression, even if it is more seamless platforming than discrete brick-breaker stages.
Excellent match for the brick-breaking half: skill shots, power-ups, escalating stage gimmicks, and boss fights, but it lacks the anchor's exploratory puzzle-adventure wrapper.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Twin Breaker: A Sacred Symbols Adventure
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Twin Breaker: A Sacred Symbols Adventure
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Brick-breaker combat is the core loop, but it is framed as a structured sci-fi campaign with progression, stages, bosses, and exploration between encounters.
Pinball-like ricochet mechanics are embedded in a dungeon-crawling, puzzle-gated adventure with traversal, unlocks, and environmental interaction.

current_live_neighbors.title: Vampire: The Masquerade - Coteries of New York

title: Super Mario Bros. Wonder - Nintendo Switch 2 Edition + Meetup in Bellabel Park

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The DLC/bundle title itself provides no standalone gameplay description, but DLC guidance applies and the parent game has strong curated gameplay evidence. Super Mario Bros. Wonder is explicitly described as 'classic Mario side-scrolling gameplay' with courses, platforming-focused progression, power-ups, enemies, and local co-op. That strongly supports action_platformer over precision_platformer: the core identity is broad Mario-style stage clearing and platform navigation rather than ultra-strict fail-heavy precision challenges. 'Meetup in Bellabel Park' appears additive and non-contradictory, so the parent game's core gameplay identity is inherited for this Switch 2 Edition bundle.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: This appears to be the parent game, providing the core 2D Mario level-based platforming, power-ups, and Wonder-effect-driven stage design.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: New Super Mario Bros. U Deluxe
staged_neighbors.requested_title: New Super Mario Bros. U Deluxe
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another modern 2D Mario built around precise side-scrolling stages, familiar power-ups, secrets, and local co-op-friendly progression.
Shares the same 2D Mario movement, enemy interactions, and course-by-course structure, though with a creation focus layered on top.
A premier modern side-scrolling platformer with tightly designed stages, collectible hunting, layered challenge, and strong co-op appeal.
A polished 2D platformer comp with imaginative stage gimmicks, smooth co-op play, and constant mechanical variety across short levels.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Copa City as a football-event tycoon/management game rather than a play-on-the-pitch sports title: 'economic strategy,' 'first football tycoon,' 'build in the city,' 'manage its structures and stadium,' and provide 'entertainment, food, and security' for fans, with success measured by 'Match Readiness.' The player organizes high-stakes football events, prepares urban space and stadium infrastructure, and manages club-specific fan behaviors and safety/fun tradeoffs. This strongly supports a management/tycoon identity with city-building and simulation strategy elements, using soccer as the theme rather than the primary play loop. Current taxonomy hints toward sports_sim are explained by football/match terminology, but the concrete gameplay loop is build/manage/optimize operations around matches, not directly competing in them.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very solid fit on build-manage venue operations, guest satisfaction, crowd handling, services, and tycoon structure. Theme park specifics differ, but the gameplay loop is closely aligned.
Strong overlap in city infrastructure, traffic, utilities, and planning, which maps well to matchday urban preparation. Less aligned on event-specific operations and contained venue management.
Shares accessible management-sim structure, facility placement, staffing, and service optimization. The institutional cadence is looser and less centered on crowd logistics or major event readiness.
Guest-oriented park management with strong emphasis on layout efficiency, amenities, crowd movement, and behind-the-scenes service logistics.
Venue-style management balancing guest needs, safety risks, infrastructure, and operational preparedness around high-attendance attractions.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store-page and Metacritic text consistently describe a first-person, short-form narrative horror game built around exploring forest and dreamscape environments while using ranged and melee weapons to survive. The copy explicitly emphasizes psychological horror, fighting to survive, upgradeable weapons, and a permadeath system that adds tension to encounters, which together support survival_horror more than purely psychological or walking-sim horror. The experience also appears authored, single-player, and linear rather than open-ended or match-based.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on first-person psychological horror, authored story progression, surreal spaces, and regular combat presence; more puzzle/boss-structured than Songbird but still a strong gameplay neighbor.

Excellent match on first-person survival pressure, vulnerability, resource scarcity, and escape-driven horror, though its semi-open systemic sandbox design is less cinematic and authored than Songbird.

Good overlap in first-person narrative horror, rural isolation, exploration, and intermittent combat, but it blends thriller/adventure elements and dual-timeline storytelling more heavily than Songbird’s tighter survival-horror loop.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Resident Evil 7: Biohazard - End of Zoe
staged_neighbors.why_similar: First-person survival horror with authored progression, resource pressure, combat, and strong narrative pacing.
First-person horror campaign mixing exploration, shooting, puzzles, and a tightly guided single-player structure.

removed_candidates: Resident Evil 7: Biohazard - End of Zoe

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence is insufficient for classification. There is no gameplay description, genre/theme/mode labeling, review text, or store copy—only the title, release date, and a platform label (PC). Title-only evidence is not enough to infer a stable gameplay archetype in Taxonomy V2.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Definitive digital board-strategy adaptation with deep turn-based engine building and long-form strategic planning.
Board-game-style strategy with faction asymmetry, economic buildup, and deliberate turn-based decision making.
Digital board game hybrid blending map movement, cards, dice, and turn-based tactical choices.
Recognizable digital tabletop adaptation built around clean turn structure and board-position planning.
Accessible digital board adaptation focused on tile placement, scoring optimization, and turn-based board control.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently establish a hybrid 'hero-driven strategy rogue-lite' with procedural runs, solo or 3-player co-op, build synergies, troop summoning, city rebuilding, quests, bosses, and meta-style power growth. However, the evidence does not clearly resolve the game’s core playable form within the allowed archetypes: it could read as action-roguelite, RTS/strategy, or tactical hero-command play, and key identity axes such as perspective and primary control/interface are not concretely stated. Because the taxonomy lacks a clean action-strategy roguelite archetype and the available text does not strongly disambiguate toward any single allowed archetype, classification would be too speculative.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on active hero combat plus base building, economy, tech progression, and defending against massive waves; less roguelite/run-structured than the anchor, but the core action-strategy blend is strong.
Matches the hero-controlled kingdom building, economy-versus-defense decisions, and wave-survival loop well. Lighter and more minimalist, and lacks the anchor's clearer roguelite/co-op breadth.

Excellent fit for rebuilding a bastion, hero progression, horde defense, and roguelite repetition, but its turn-based tactical RPG identity is a meaningful mismatch from the anchor's likely real-time hero-strategy feel.

Understandable comp via roguelite structure, hero/team management, procedural progression, and defending a core objective against swarms, but its room-based dungeon-defense format is looser than the anchor's kingdom-rebuild loop.
Direct-control co-op hero play fused with village upgrading, night wave defense, boss goals, and run-based progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Étrange Overlord as a frenetic real-time action RPG: players 'shoot, dodge, and slash' through battlefields, use a unique revolving-lane combat mechanic, and command from a roster of 15+ playable allies in 2-4 player co-op. That supports a combat-dominant, fast, stage-oriented action identity more than RPG subgenres centered on loot, open worlds, or party command from an overhead perspective. The text includes musical framing and songs, but it does not clearly establish rhythm-timing as the core gameplay loop; the current computed rhythm/party signals appear driven by thematic wording rather than concrete mechanics. Overall the strongest fit is character_action, with some beat-em-up overlap due to multiplayer crowd-fighting emphasis.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest fit here: stylish real-time character action built around music-synced combat and encounter flow. Less party/co-op/RPG-driven than the anchor, but much nearer in moment-to-moment feel than the grid-based rhythm titles.

A reasonable neighbor through frenetic music-centered action and boss/stage structure. It is lighter on party management, progression, and multiplayer identity, so it fits more as an adjacent action-music comp.
Music-driven action combat with 1-4 player co-op, loot/progression, and constant real-time battlefield pressure.
Co-op action RPG with character roster, side-scrolling crowd fights, builds, and replayable stage progression.
Arcade-style 4-player action RPG with light progression, chaotic melee combat, and strong local/online co-op identity.

removed_candidates: Cadence of Hyrule: Crypt of the NecroDancer Featuring The Legend of Zelda

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Screamer as an 'arcade racing game' centered on tournament-based car races in a futuristic anime-styled setting. Core loop evidence is strong and repeated across Steam and Metacritic: race on tracks, use vehicles with unique abilities, juggle Boost/Strike/Shield, compete in multiple race modes including Team Races, and play online or split-screen. The dominant identity is vehicular racing with arcade combat embellishments rather than a fighting game proper, supporting arcade_racer over party_game or realistic_racer.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: futuristic high-speed arcade racing built around weapons, shields/turbos, combat pressure, and multiplayer race modes.
Excellent combat-arcade racer comp with boost and defensive/offensive powers in pack racing, though it is more pickup-driven and less character-ability-based.
Strong on arcade speed, takedowns, and team-based competition, but it de-emphasizes traditional finish-order racing structure compared with Screamer.
Arcade team-racing focus with character-linked handling/abilities and modes where teamwork matters beyond pure finishing position.
High-speed futuristic anti-grav racing centered on boost management, weapon combat, and skillful track mastery.

current_live_neighbors.title: Monster Energy Supercross 25 - The Official Video Game

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify a single-player, third-person, story-driven Life is Strange game centered on alternating between Max and Chloe, making consequential dialogue decisions, rewinding time, investigating suspects, and solving environmental/four-dimensional puzzles. However, that evidence points most strongly to a narrative adventure / choice-driven adventure identity that is not represented cleanly in the allowed archetype list. The game does not show sufficient evidence for visual_novel, cinematic_action_adventure, immersive_sim_action, or other available archetypes without forcing a poor fit. Because the core gameplay identity is clear but falls into a taxonomy gap rather than an existing supported archetype, this title should be rejected instead of misclassified.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest match: Max-led branching story adventure with rewind-based choice revision, exploration, and puzzle solving.
Direct modern predecessor built around Max, supernatural investigation, dialogue choices, and timeline-altering narrative play.

staged_neighbors.requested_title: Life is Strange: Before the Storm

Episodic-style narrative adventure emphasizing dialogue choices, character perspectives, light puzzles, and supernatural-tinged mystery.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence confirms this is a compilation with single-player and online PvP support, battles, Bonus Cards, and some narrative setup around Geo Stelar and Omega-Xis. However, the provided DB text does not concretely establish the core gameplay loop, perspective, exploration structure, combat format, or progression model strongly enough to map it to a supported V2 archetype. 'Battle Cards' suggests some card-based system, but the evidence does not show whether cards are the primary play structure versus an action RPG, tactical RPG, or hybrid system. Because the bundle lacks sufficiently specific gameplay identity signals, classification would be too speculative.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Mega Man Battle Network Legacy Collection
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Mega Man Battle Network Legacy Collection
staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest comp: another Mega Man collection built around exploration, progression, and real-time grid/card combat with deck customization and online legacy features. Extremely overlapping audience and play identity.

Very close on moment-to-moment combat feel: real-time lane-based dodging plus deck/spell sequencing. It diverges through roguelike run structure and heavier bullet-hell emphasis, but the combat DNA is highly relevant.

title: SHINONOME ABYSS The Maiden Exorcist

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently establish a horror-themed single-player roguelike about escaping procedurally changing haunted mansions using limited items, traps, environmental hazards, clue-reading, and survival across three modes including fixed-map puzzle scenarios and endless survival. However, the evidence does not cleanly anchor the game to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It suggests a hybrid of horror action, survival, puzzle-solving, and roguelike structure, but key archetype-defining signals are missing or contradictory: no reliable perspective/view, no clear combat model beyond trap use and occasional empowered state, and no strong fit to survival_horror, action_horror, psychological_horror, turn_based_tactics, or immersive_sim_action as defined by the available taxonomy. Because the gameplay identity appears materially hybrid and the closest archetypes are only partial matches, classification would be too speculative.

Very close on turn-based procedural expeditions, limited resources, repeated runs, and survival pressure, though it leans more into character-building and combat than mansion escape/trap play.
Shares room-based procedural progression, enemy prediction, environmental interactions, and careful tactical pacing; more overtly combat-centric and less survival-horror than the anchor.
Good horror-action neighbor for evasion, reading threats, and navigating spirit-filled spaces, but it lacks the anchor's roguelike run structure, trap/resource systems, and procedural replay loop.

title: DAMON and BABY

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as an action-adventure centered on exploration plus highly technical twin-stick shooting, alternating between an exploration phase and a home-base upgrade/shop phase, with weapon pickups, skill customization, boss battles, and optional local co-op. However, that gameplay identity does not cleanly fit the allowed archetype set: it is not a beat 'em up, and the evidence is too mixed for open_world_action_adventure because the copy also frames progression around clearing stages/areas and boss encounters rather than a clearly quest-web open world structure. The result is a strong description of the game, but not a strong enough match to an existing taxonomy archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on twin-stick shooting, exploration through dense zones, loot, builds, and co-op. More RPG/quest-heavy and cover-based than the anchor, but still the strongest gameplay match.

Excellent top-down shooter comp with co-op, weapon variety, character growth, area clearing, and big combat focus. Less exploration/adventure-oriented than the anchor, but the combat loop is very close.
Shares overhead perspective, action-adventure progression, boss fights, exploration, and co-op. However, it is more melee-forward, puzzle/adventure-driven, and less twin-stick-shooter-centric.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Lara Croft and the Temple of Osiris
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Lara Croft and the Temple of Osiris
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Area-based exploration and twin-stick combat paired with local co-op, progression through stages, and boss-focused encounters.
Highly technical twin-stick shooting with distinct stages, major boss fights, and strong local co-op emphasis.

unresolved_similar_games: Lara Croft and the Guardian of Light

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify a top-down retro pixel-art single-player survival shooter focused on surviving enemy waves in a confined arena, collecting XP drops, auto-attacking with acquired weapons, and gaining stronger odds through repeated failed attempts. This is strong evidence for the game's gameplay identity, but that identity is a survivorlike / bullet-heaven style auto-attacking horde survival game, which is not represented by the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list. Available archetypes such as arena_fps, loot_shooter, action_horror, co_op_action_roguelite, or extraction_shooter would materially misclassify the core loop.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Defines the horde-survival loop of movement-focused dodging, automatic attacks, XP pickups, mid-run weapon upgrades, and escalating enemy waves.
The prior game in the same series is the clearest direct comp if this sequel keeps the same top-down arena survival and weapon-stacking structure.
Another compact arena survivor built around surviving timed waves, stacking weapons, and making rapid build choices between runs.
Shares the auto-attacking bullet-heaven format with large enemy hordes, frequent level-up choices, and run-based power growth.
Very similar top-down retro-styled survival combat with kiting, auto-triggered offense, XP collection, and surviving until wave objectives are cleared.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored gameplay text is sufficient and consistent for a metroidvania classification. The Steam detailed description explicitly describes a 2D platformer structure with combat and ability-gated exploration: Ariana defeats enemies to earn new magic spells and abilities that grant access to previously unreachable areas, takes on platforming and combat challenges, and fights formidable bosses. This strongly supports side-scrolling traversal, platform navigation, boss-driven combat, and progression via new abilities opening the map over time, which is the core identity of metroidvania. The seven codices framing suggests segmented but exploratory spaces rather than purely linear stages, so semi_open is the best fit. Secondary action_platformer is also supported, but the explicit unreachable-area gating and ability unlock loop make metroidvania the stronger primary archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Side-scrolling action-platforming with magic ability unlocks, backtracking to newly reachable areas, and a light, character-driven adventure structure.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: ENDER LILIES: Quietus of the Knights
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Ender Lilies: Quietus of the Knights
staged_neighbors.why_similar: 2D combat-platformer built around collecting abilities, equipping different skill loadouts, tough bosses, and ability-gated progression.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Record of Lodoss War: Deedlit in Wonder Labyrinth
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Record of Lodoss War: Deedlit in Wonder Labyrinth
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Anime-styled 2D action-platforming with precision boss fights, magic-focused combat, and exploration that expands through newly gained movement/combat powers.
Fast 2D action-platforming with RPG-style stat growth, crafted/equipped upgrades, and combat-heavy progression through interconnected areas.
Bullet-heavy 2D action-platformer featuring demanding boss encounters, movement upgrades, and progression shaped by platforming skill and character build choices.
